sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Hypovolaemic shock is life-threatening circulatory failure caused by reduced intravascular volume. It is most commonly due to haemorrhage but may also follow major fluid loss from burns, vomiting, diarrhoea, or third-space losses. Early recognition and cause-directed treatment are critical.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Tachycardia, tachypnoea, cool peripheries, weak pulse, pallor, or delayed capillary refill
- Altered mental state, anxiety, or reduced conscious level
- Hypotension is a late and concerning sign
- Reduced urine output may be present if known
- In trauma, assume occult haemorrhage until proven otherwise

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Shock severity cannot be judged by blood pressure alone
- Young, fit, pregnant, medicated, or elderly patients may compensate differently
- Haemorrhagic and non-haemorrhagic hypovolaemia are not managed identically
- Minimise scene time in time-critical cases and continue management during transport where feasible
- Prevent hypothermia and address the underlying cause early
- In traumatic shock, haemorrhage control and rapid access to definitive care usually take priority over aggressive crystalloid administration

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Control life-threatening external bleeding immediately
- Perform primary survey and frequent reassessment
- Position the patient according to injury pattern, airway needs, and tolerance rather than automatically elevating the legs
- Keep the patient warm and minimise further heat loss
- Give oxygen only when clinically indicated
- Expedite access to definitive care when the patient is time critical

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Obtain vascular access when it can be done without delaying more important interventions
- Use fluid therapy judiciously according to the likely cause, the patient response, and any competing priorities such as traumatic brain injury
- Consider point-of-care testing where available and useful, but do not delay treatment for it
- Provide analgesia and antiemetic therapy as appropriate within the broader resuscitation plan
- Pre-notify the receiving service for time-critical shock or major haemorrhage

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- In traumatic haemorrhagic shock, early haemorrhage control and rapid transfer are often more important than large-volume crystalloid administration
- In non-haemorrhagic fluid loss, more liberal fluid replacement may be appropriate depending on the clinical context
